primary_diagnosis.name: Acute anxiety/panic response to an incidental early autoimmune gastritis (parietal-cell antibody) result

comorbidities.name: Early autoimmune (atrophic) gastritis with mildly reduced B12 absorption

pathophysiology_summary: Autoimmune (parietal-cell antibody) gastritis is a slow process that thins the gastric body mucosa over years and can mildly reduce B12 absorption; it is typically asymptomatic and managed with B12 monitoring/replacement and surveillance. Here, mis-reading the antibody result triggered a panic attack with tachycardia, nausea, epigastric tightness and hyperventilation. The genuine mimics to screen are an acute upper GI bleed (haematemesis/coffee-ground vomit/melaena/postural syncope) and ACS (exertional/radiating chest pain) — neither confirmable remotely — after which a classic low-risk panic picture can be de-escalated with a safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Rest onset locked to reading the result, hyperventilation, nausea and prior health-anxiety episodes.
Given the patient's fear and gastric pathology, screen for haematemesis/melaena/postural syncope.
Tachycardia and chest tightness warrant a brief cardiac screen even in a younger patient.
Screen for epigastric point pain/NSAID use; not the picture here.
No UGIB features or postural syncope (DI-001), non-exertional rest-onset tightness with hyperventilation (DI-002), trigger-locked onset with prior episodes (DI-003) — a classic panic attack.
No haematemesis/melaena/postural syncope — no evidence of active bleeding, but retain a safety-net.
Non-exertional, non-radiating, rest onset after a trigger — low ACS probability with a retained safety-net.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: brief UGIB/ACS screen, confident de-escalation of a low-risk panic attack with paced breathing, explanation that the antibody finding is non-urgent (B12 monitoring), and a UGIB/ACS safety-net. Both reflex ambulance and unscreened dismissal fail.

red_flags.description: Haematemesis/coffee-ground vomit/melaena or postural syncope (acute UGIB)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate active upper GI bleeding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed UGIB can cause haemorrhagic shock.
A missed ACS behind an anxiety label can be fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling as panic without screening UGIB/ACS.
Being pulled into the 'internal bleeding/cancer' fear and dispatching an ambulance for a low-risk panic attack.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Examination and bloods (Hb) to exclude occult bleeding
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: If red-flag negative and low-risk: paced breathing, reassurance and a UGIB/ACS safety-net with routine GP follow-up for B12 monitoring.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: False reassurance could miss UGIB/ACS; over-triage reinforces catastrophic health anxiety.
